lens: DECIDABILITY AND EXPRESSIVENESS — honest rule recount at kernel-spec granularity, one-pass/no-smuggled-flow verification, and week-one systems idiom stress

findings.title: Honest rule count is ~26, not 12 — roughly 2x the 15 budget and at the >25 automatic-fail line
findings.detail: Recounting each independently enforceable normative statement at OWN-1..OWN-13 granularity: TOK-1 = 4 (confined declaration/affinity/region-parameterization; confined field discipline; ban on borrow/confined fields in non-confined types; opaque form-table types + no user literals), TOK-2 = 2 (placement whitelist; sealed-form type-parameter instantiation ban — a generic-instantiation check, not a value-placement check), TOK-3 = 2 (region instantiation at creation; outlives/return escape restriction), TOK-4 = 2 (affine move with loan renaming + destination constraint; statement-scoped & of confined bindings), LOAN-1 = 3 (state object + closed update set; legality→consume→issue phase order; function-entry signature-implied loans), LOAN-2 = 3 (literal issue; call issue via region unification; declaration-time exactly-one-borrow-occurrence check — a separate declarations-pass meta-rule), LOAN-3 = 2 (unique exclusion; consume-exception), LOAN-4 = 1, LOAN-5 = 2 (own-consume + intra-statement ordering; scope-end auto-consume + reverse drop order), LOAN-6 = 1, LOAN-7 = 2 (brand recording; call-site brand check), LOAN-8 = 2 (match join; loop entry=exit + break states). Total: 26. The claimed 12 is achieved only by packing 2-4 norms per numbered paragraph. Worse, the frozen derivations already depend on rules that are not written at all (confined-field projection for P6, match-arm token extraction, &uniq minting on confined receivers for P3, derived-drop legality, the LOAN-8 state projection, region-keyed source maps, generic-instantiation confinement checking); writing the spec the derivations actually use pushes the count to ~30-32. Under the exercise's own terms (<=15 required, >25 automatic FAIL) the draft as written is out of budget and the completed design crosses the auto-fail line.

findings.title: LOAN-8's literal state comparison rejects the frozen ACCEPTs P4 and A10 — the comparison domain and state projection are undefined
findings.detail: LOAN-8 compares 'the same loan sets, source maps, and consumed-flags' — restricted to 'bindings live at the join' for match arms, but 'exactly the loan state it began in' for loop bodies and 'that same state' for breaks with no domain restriction at all. At A10's break, body-local confined e is lexically in scope with consumed-flag true; the loop-entry state has no binding e, so the states are not identical under any literal reading and A10 REJECTS. P4's Done-arm break likewise occurs with body-local p in scope (and the Wait arm's body-end state carries moved-out g2 before scope exit). The derivations silently apply a projection the rules never define: state = nonempty loan sets plus source maps of live UNCONSUMED confined bindings, compared after arm/body scope-end drops. That projection must itself be a normative rule (more budget); without it the deliverable's rule text contradicts two frozen ACCEPT verdicts, and with the maximal reading it rejects every loop whose body binds anything. This is not smuggled flow analysis — it is a missing definition — but rule text is the deliverable, and the rule text as written fails its own canonical programs.

findings.title: TOK-4 move into an earlier consumed binding falsifies LOAN-5's reverse-drop-order claim — derived drops of loaned sources are unlegislated, and the permissive reading dangles a token in an accepted program
findings.detail: TOK-4 permits moving into 'an existing binding of identical type whose value has been consumed' — the exact mechanism P4's g = g2 requires — and identical type is satisfiable across two sources in one block (same block region). The move puts a live token into a binding declared BEFORE its source, so LOAN-5's justification 'reverse binding order, so same-block tokens are consumed before their sources are dropped' is simply false. Nothing legislates the derived drop of t2: LOAN-3 governs statements (scope end is not one) and LOAN-6 says 'explicitly dropped', which a compiler-derived drop is not. Strict reading: the checker re-applies LOAN-6 at derived drops and rejects at the closing brace (sound, but a new rule with odd diagnostics). Permissive reading: an ACCEPTED program executes a token drop obligation against freed storage — exactly the dangle the soundness sketch claims impossible. Cheap fixes exist (delete 'explicitly' from LOAN-6 and state that derived drops are subject to it, or constrain TOK-4 destinations to bindings declared after every source in the moved value's source map — both lexically checkable), but each is a new normative clause, and the spec is currently ambiguous precisely where the soundness sketch asserts certainty.

findings.title: P6 depends on confined-field projection that no rule types (and confined-in-confined construction/consumption is likewise unspecified)
findings.detail: P6 is a frozen ACCEPT whose body reads env.t and calls get on the projected borrow. Under the kernel's no-reborrow theorem T-A, producing a usable borrow from a stored field is exactly the operation the draft's rules must license and bound, and none does: is projection statement-scoped, shared-only, allowed through & env but not own env? For &uniq fields it must be restricted (a writer env confined struct W<'d>{ d: &uniq 'd buffer } projected in two statements would duplicate a unique borrow), but nothing written restricts it. The same gap covers confined-in-confined fields, which TOK-1 explicitly permits: constructing Pair { e: e0 } must rename e0's held loans onto the pair (TOK-4 only covers binding-to-binding moves) and define the pair's composite source map for LOAN-7; projecting or consuming the pair is undefined. P6 is currently underivable from the rules as written; fixing it costs 1-2 rules that are themselves a limited reborrow mechanism and therefore need hostile review, not just a clause.

findings.title: Try-acquire retry loops are unwritable: no confinement polymorphism for option, no match-extraction rule for token-carrying enums, and LOAN-8 forbids breaking while holding — yet the frozen mutex/queue corpus contains this idiom
findings.detail: Three stacked walls. (i) option<guard<'m>> instantiates a non-confined enum with a confined field — ill-formed by TOK-1 — and the design has no confinement polymorphism, so every fallible token operation (try_lock, try_pop, try_recv, entry-probe as an enum) needs a bespoke confined enum declared in the form table. (ii) Matching such an enum extracts a confined value into an arm binding; TOK-2 permits the position but no LOAN rule transfers the scrutinee's held loans to the arm binding. (iii) Even granting (i) and (ii), exiting the loop while holding the token is a LOAN-8 state mismatch with no legal restructure: the token cannot be consumed before break (it is the loop's product), the issuing op cannot be hoisted (it is the thing being retried), and no pre-loop destination binding can exist consumed at the right type. Per-iteration acquire-use-release (A10 shape) works; acquire-then-exit does not. Spin/backoff acquisition over mutexes and queue endpoints is core to the frozen mimalloc/Crossbeam operations, and the only detours — a blocking form op, or moving the whole retry loop inside the sealed form — mean the language cannot express its own corpus's acquire idiom in user code. This is the design's largest genuine expressiveness hole, distinct from the self-reported multi-token coarseness.

findings.title: &uniq statement-scoped minting on confined bindings is unstated but load-bearing for P3's c.next() and every cursor-advance/guard-mutate op
findings.detail: TOK-4's only borrow clause for confined bindings covers `&`; P3's derivation calls the &uniq receiver mint on c 'legal' with no rule. Kernel statement-scoped minting is defined for owner bindings, and a confined binding is an affine owner of its value, so the intent is clear — but T-A says &uniq borrow values are consumed by the calls that receive them, and nothing states how that composes with a confined receiver whose held loans must survive the call. One sentence fixes it ('statement-scoped mints of either mode from a confined binding are permitted and transfer no loans'), but it is a hidden rule for the recount, and without it P3's step S3 and every cursor.next()/guard-mutation op is underivable.

findings.title: Multi-token composites resolve only into fat form ops, and LOAN-4's missing consume-exception (asymmetric with LOAN-3) silently forces all mutating-consume tokens to unique strength — mostly acceptable detours, but unowned in the rule text
findings.detail: Verified working week-one shapes, for the record: wordcount via probe(&e)/fill(own e)/incr(own e) with per-iteration LOAN-8 balance (A10 shape); buffered-read fill_buf loop via consume_n(own buf, n) in the continue arm and discard(own buf) before break (the A7 per-arm-consume discipline); guard passed to a helper by & (TOK-4), and own-and-return helpers by threading &'m mutex through the signature, which LOAN-2b/LOAN-7 handle exactly as designed; LRU promote via unique-strength find_mut then promote(own c) under LOAN-3's consume-exception; two-cursor zip-merge inline via A4, and as a helper by threading the source borrow. Residue: (a) any two simultaneously live unique tokens on one binding (get2, swap, entry+cursor) is unwritable except as a new sealed two-key op — each composite grows the trusted form table the minimality doctrine wants small (draft self-reports; confirmed real, judged acceptable short-term); (b) the LOAN-3/LOAN-4 asymmetry means an op consuming a SHARED token cannot take &uniq of the source in the same statement, so delete-capable cursors must be unique-strength from issue — semantically right for rebalancing structures, but the asymmetry is a design decision the rules never state or defend, and writers will discover it as an inexplicable rejection.

findings.title: State-shape and meta-rule gaps: LOAN-7 needs region-keyed source maps that LOAN-1 does not define, and generic-instantiation confinement checking is unstated
findings.detail: LOAN-1 defines the source map as (source binding -> strength), but LOAN-7's check is 'contains exactly B at 'p' — a region-keyed lookup ('p -> (binding, strength)). For single-region tokens the difference is invisible; for multi-region confined results, which LOAN-2b explicitly supports ('for each region parameter of the result type', e.g. a join cursor over two tables), the region-less map cannot answer LOAN-7's question. Separately, TOK-1/TOK-2 bans (confined field in non-confined type; confined instantiation of sealed-form type parameters) say nothing about USER generic parameters: fn f<T>(x: own T) or struct Box<T>{v:T} instantiated at entry<'t> must be caught somewhere. Post-monomorphization re-checking is the kernel's natural answer and does catch A2-style wrappers, but the rules must say when confinement is checked or A2's reasoning is incomplete for generic code. Both are one-clause fixes; both add to the recount.

verdict: Not sound and not within budget as written — but the failures are spec incompleteness and count dishonesty, not smuggled flow analysis. The architecture itself passes the decidability attack cleanly: per-binding lexical loan sets, signature-derived issue/consume with a declarations-pass uniqueness check (table lookup, no call-site search), binding-identity brands as set-membership tests, and equality-instead-of-fixpoint joins are genuinely one syntax-directed pass, and most week-one idioms have blessed shapes (wordcount, buffered-read, LRU promote, zip-merge, and guard-helper threading all verified writable in the draft's own terms). What fails is the deliverable: the honest rule count is ~26 against a claimed 12 (budget 15, auto-fail 25), and it rises to ~30 once the rules the frozen derivations already lean on are actually written — confined-field projection (P6 is underivable today), match-arm token extraction, the LOAN-8 state projection (whose literal text rejects frozen ACCEPTs P4 and A10), derived-drop legality (without which a TOK-4 move into an earlier consumed binding dangles a token in an accepted program), and &uniq minting on confined receivers (P3's next()). The single change that matters most: define the loan-state object once, explicitly — nonempty loan sets plus region-keyed source maps of live unconsumed confined bindings, compared after scope-end drops — and re-derive join agreement, breaks, derived drops, moves, and projection from that one definition; it simultaneously repairs the P4/A10 contradiction and the drop-order soundness hole, and it forces the honest recount that decides whether this mechanism fits in <=15 kernel-granularity rules or whether the budget itself is the finding to report upward. Separately, the try-acquire loop needs a designed answer rather than a detour, because the frozen mutex/queue corpus contains that idiom and the current rules cannot express it in user code.
